================================================================================
MASTER FARMING ADVISORY DATASET - FARMER ASSIST RAG KNOWLEDGE BASE
================================================================================
This single comprehensive dataset supports an AI-powered Smart Farming Advisory
Agent covering 22 crops across soil/climate suitability, growing seasons, pest
and disease control, market prices, weather forecasts, fertilizer guidance,
government schemes, and general farming reference information.

Data compiled and paraphrased from: Crop Recommendation Dataset (soil/climate
samples), ICAR institute advisories and Krishi Vigyan Kendra guides (pest and
disease control), Agmarknet/data.gov.in (market prices, mid-2026 sample
snapshot), IMD All India Weather Bulletin dated 11 July 2026 (weather), and
general Package of Practices references (fertilizer dosage, government schemes).

IMPORTANT LIMITATIONS:
- Weather and mandi price data are STATIC SNAPSHOTS for demonstration purposes.
  A production deployment should replace these with live API feeds (IMD, Agmarknet).
- Fertilizer dosage figures are general starting guidance; actual application
  should be adjusted using the farmer's own Soil Health Card report.
- Government scheme details (amounts, eligibility) should be verified at the
  official scheme websites before a farmer relies on them for a decision.
- This dataset is a reference for an AI advisory demo project and is not a
  substitute for a licensed agronomist or official government/IMD sources.

SEASON DEFINITIONS: Kharif = monsoon crops (Jun-Oct). Rabi = winter crops
(Nov-Mar). Zaid = short summer crops (Apr-May). Perennial = tree/vine crops
not tied to a single season.

TABLE OF CONTENTS:
Section A: Full crop recommendation dataset (2200 individual soil/climate samples)
Section B: Expanded pest and disease control reference (multiple entries per crop)
Section C: Multi-market mandi price reference (3 markets per crop)
Section D: Full 7-day rainfall forecast table (36 meteorological subdivisions)
Section E: Government schemes and subsidies for farmers
Section F: Fertilizer dosage general guidance (by crop)
Section G: Glossary of agricultural terms
Section H: Frequently asked questions (FAQ)
Section I: General weather summary and regional agromet advisories
Section J: Weather terminology reference
Section K: Pest control quick reference summary (condensed, one entry per crop)

================================================================================
SECTION A: FULL CROP RECOMMENDATION DATASET - DETAILED INDIVIDUAL SOIL/CLIMATE SAMPLES
================================================================================
Each entry below is one real recorded sample of soil nutrients and climate conditions under
which the named crop was grown successfully, including how this specific sample compares to
the typical range observed for that crop. There are 100 samples per crop across 22 crops
(2200 total samples), useful for answering detailed soil-suitability questions for any crop.

--- Apple: 100 recorded soil/climate samples ---
Apple record #1: In this recorded sample, soil Nitrogen level was 29 units (on the higher end of the typical range for Apple), Phosphorus level was 144 units (on the higher end of the typical range), and Potassium level was 204 units (on the higher end of the typical range). The climate was moderate at 22.43 degrees Celsius with 92.49% relative humidity. The soil was mildly acidic at pH 5.8, and the site received moderate rainfall of 119.1 mm (on the higher end of the typical range for Apple). Apple was successfully cultivated under this specific combination of soil nutrients and climate.

The climate was moderate at 26.49 degrees Celsius with 88.59% relative humidity. The soil was mildly acidic at pH 6.31, and the site received low rainfall of 46.06 mm (on the lower end of the typical range for Watermelon). Watermelon was successfully cultivated under this specific combination of soil nutrients and climate.

================================================================================
SECTION B: EXPANDED PEST AND DISEASE CONTROL REFERENCE (MULTIPLE ENTRIES PER CROP)
================================================================================
Compiled and paraphrased from ICAR institute advisories, Krishi Vigyan Kendra guides,
and general crop protection references. Includes both insect pests and fungal/bacterial diseases.

--- Rice ---
Issue: Yellow Stem Borer (insect pest)
Symptoms: Larvae tunnel into the stem, causing 'dead heart' in young plants and 'white ear' in mature plants.
Control measures: Install light traps to monitor moth activity. Release Trichogramma egg parasitoids as biological control. Apply Chlorantraniliprole-based insecticide if infestation crosses threshold levels.

Issue: Brown Planthopper (insect pest)
Symptoms: Sap-sucking insect causing yellowing and drying, leading to 'hopper burn' patches in the field.
Control measures: Avoid excess nitrogen fertilizer. Maintain proper field drainage. Use resistant rice varieties. Apply insecticide only if population exceeds economic threshold.

Issue: Blast disease (fungal)
Symptoms: Causes diamond-shaped lesions on leaves, and can infect nodes and panicles, leading to significant yield loss.
Control measures: Use blast-resistant varieties. Avoid excess nitrogen application. Apply Tricyclazole-based fungicide at early symptom onset.

--- Maize ---
Issue: Fall Armyworm (insect pest)
Symptoms: Larvae feed on whorl leaves, creating window-pane damage and ragged holes; can also damage cobs.
Control measures: Intercrop with legumes (pigeonpea, cowpea, black gram). Install pheromone traps soon after sowing. Encourage bird perches. Apply Bt or Spinetoram-based insecticide if damage crosses threshold.

Issue: Maize Stem Borer (insect pest)
Symptoms: Larvae bore into the stem causing 'dead heart' symptoms similar to rice stem borer.
Control measures: Remove and destroy affected plants early. Apply neem-based formulations at early infestation. Release Trichogramma for biological control.

Issue: Turcicum Leaf Blight (fungal)
Symptoms: Long, elliptical grey-green lesions on leaves that can coalesce and reduce photosynthetic area.
Control measures: Use resistant hybrids. Practice crop rotation. Apply Mancozeb-based fungicide if severe.

--- Chickpea ---
Issue: Gram Pod Borer (insect pest)
Symptoms: Larvae bore into developing pods, feeding on seeds and causing significant yield loss.
Control measures: Monitor with pheromone traps. Encourage natural predators. Apply NPV biopesticide or insecticide at early larval stage.

Issue: Fusarium Wilt (fungal)
Symptoms: Causes yellowing and wilting of the whole plant, often visible as sudden drying of a patch in the field.
Control measures: Use wilt-resistant varieties. Practice crop rotation with cereals. Treat seeds with Trichoderma-based bio-fungicide before sowing.

--- Kidneybeans ---
Issue: Aphids and Whitefly (insect pest)
Symptoms: Sap-sucking pests causing leaf curling, yellowing, and reduced pod formation; can transmit viral diseases.
Control measures: Use yellow sticky traps for monitoring. Spray neem-based formulations. Encourage natural predators like ladybird beetles.

Issue: Bean Common Mosaic Virus
Symptoms: Causes mottled yellow-green patterns on leaves and stunted plant growth, spread mainly by aphids.
Control measures: Use certified virus-free seed. Control aphid vectors early. Remove and destroy infected plants promptly.

--- Pigeonpeas ---
Issue: Gram Pod Borer and Pod Fly (insect pest)
Symptoms: Pod borer larvae feed inside pods; pod fly maggots damage developing seeds.
Control measures: Use pheromone traps for pod borer monitoring. Practice timely sowing to avoid peak pest periods. Apply insecticide at pod formation if severe.

Issue: Fusarium Wilt (fungal)
Symptoms: Causes progressive yellowing and wilting, often starting from lower leaves upward.
Control measures: Use wilt-resistant varieties. Avoid continuous pigeonpea cultivation in the same field. Improve field drainage.

--- Mothbeans ---
Issue: Aphids (insect pest)
Symptoms: Sap-sucking damage causing stunted growth and leaf curling; can spread viral disease.
Control measures: Spray neem oil as a natural repellent. Encourage beneficial insects. Use mild insecticide only if infestation is severe.

Issue: Yellow Mosaic Virus
Symptoms: Causes yellow mosaic patterning on leaves, spread by whitefly, reducing pod yield.
Control measures: Control whitefly vector population. Use tolerant varieties where available. Remove infected plants early.

--- Mungbean ---
Issue: Thrips and Whitefly (insect pest)
Symptoms: Thrips cause flower shedding and reduced pod set; whitefly transmits viral diseases.
Control measures: Use yellow/blue sticky traps for monitoring. Spray neem-based formulations at early infestation. Avoid water stress.

Issue: Yellow Mosaic Virus
Symptoms: Yellow-green mottling on leaves, transmitted by whitefly, a major constraint in mungbean cultivation.
Control measures: Use resistant varieties where available. Control whitefly populations. Rogue out infected plants early in the season.

--- Blackgram ---
Issue: Bihar Hairy Caterpillar and Whitefly (insect pest)
Symptoms: Caterpillars feed on foliage causing defoliation; whitefly causes yellowing and virus transmission.
Control measures: Handpick and destroy egg masses and early-stage caterpillars. Use light traps for adult moth monitoring. Apply insecticide if defoliation is severe.

Issue: Yellow Mosaic Virus
Symptoms: Similar to mungbean, causes yellow mottling and stunted growth, spread by whitefly.
Control measures: Control whitefly vector. Use tolerant varieties. Remove and destroy infected plants.

--- Lentil ---
Issue: Black Aphid (insect pest)
Symptoms: Sap-sucking pest causing leaf curling, stunted growth, and reduced pod formation.
Control measures: Encourage natural predators such as ladybird beetles and lacewings. Spray neem oil for early-stage infestation. Apply insecticide only if threshold crossed.

Issue: Rust disease (fungal)
Symptoms: Orange-brown pustules appear on leaves and stems, reducing photosynthetic capacity and yield.
Control measures: Use rust-resistant varieties. Avoid dense sowing to improve air circulation. Apply recommended fungicide at early symptom stage.

--- Pomegranate ---
Issue: Pomegranate Fruit Borer (insect pest)
Symptoms: Larvae bore into developing fruit, causing internal rot and premature fruit drop.
Control measures: Bag fruits at early development stage. Use pheromone traps for monitoring. Remove and destroy infested fruits.

Issue: Bacterial Blight
Symptoms: Causes dark, irregular leaf spots and fruit cracking, spread by rain splash and infected planting material.
Control measures: Use disease-free planting material. Prune and destroy infected twigs. Apply copper-based bactericide during wet periods.

--- Banana ---
Issue: Banana Weevil Borer and Nematodes (insect/nematode pest)
Symptoms: Weevil larvae bore into the pseudostem and rhizome; nematodes cause root and corm rot.
Control measures: Use healthy, disease-free planting material. Apply neem cake to soil around the base. Practice field sanitation. Rotate with non-host crops.

Issue: Panama Wilt (fungal)
Symptoms: Causes yellowing of older leaves progressing upward, and splitting of the pseudostem base.
Control measures: Use resistant varieties (e.g. tissue-culture plants of resistant cultivars). Avoid replanting in infected soil for several years. Improve field drainage.

--- Mango ---
Issue: Mango Hopper (insect pest)
Symptoms: Sap-sucking insect causing stunted growth, honeydew secretion, and sooty mold development.
Control measures: Prune dense canopy to improve air circulation. Spray neem oil during flowering stage. Avoid spraying during pollination to protect bees.

Issue: Anthracnose (fungal)
Symptoms: Causes dark sunken spots on fruit, leaves, and flowers, especially severe during humid weather.
Control measures: Prune for better air circulation. Apply Carbendazim or copper-based fungicide at flowering and fruit development stages.

--- Grapes ---
Issue: Mealybugs (insect pest)
Symptoms: Sap-sucking insects covered in white waxy coating, causing weakened vines and sooty mold.
Control measures: Use sticky bands around vine trunks. Encourage natural predators. Apply insecticide at bark crevices during early infestation.

Issue: Downy Mildew (fungal)
Symptoms: Yellow oily patches on upper leaf surface with white fungal growth underneath, thrives in humid conditions.
Control measures: Ensure good canopy ventilation. Apply Mancozeb or copper-based fungicide preventively during humid/rainy periods.

--- Watermelon ---
Issue: Fruit Fly (insect pest)
Symptoms: Adult flies lay eggs in developing fruit; larvae feed inside, causing rotting and premature fruit drop.
Control measures: Use methyl eugenol-based fruit fly traps. Practice field sanitation. Use bagging for individual fruits where feasible.

Issue: Fusarium Wilt (fungal)
Symptoms: Causes sudden wilting of vines, often one side of the plant first, with vascular browning inside the stem.
Control measures: Use wilt-resistant varieties. Practice crop rotation. Avoid waterlogging.

--- Muskmelon ---
Issue: Fruit Fly and Aphids (insect pest)
Symptoms: Fruit fly larvae damage developing fruit internally; aphids cause leaf curling and viral spread.
Control measures: Use fruit fly traps. Spray neem oil for aphid control. Remove and destroy infested fruits promptly.

Issue: Powdery Mildew (fungal)
Symptoms: White powdery fungal growth on leaf surfaces, reducing photosynthesis and fruit quality.
Control measures: Ensure adequate plant spacing for airflow. Apply sulfur-based fungicide at early symptom onset.

--- Apple ---
Issue: Woolly Apple Aphid (insect pest)
Symptoms: Cottony white masses on bark, roots, and shoots; causes galls and weakened tree growth.
Control measures: Prune and destroy heavily infested shoots. Encourage the parasitic wasp Aphelinus mali. Apply insecticide to bark during dormant season if severe.

Issue: Apple Scab (fungal)
Symptoms: Olive-green to black spots on leaves and fruit, causing premature leaf drop and fruit blemishes.
Control measures: Use scab-resistant varieties. Prune for air circulation. Apply preventive fungicide spray program during wet spring weather.

--- Orange ---
Issue: Citrus Leaf Miner and Fruit Borer (insect pest)
Symptoms: Leaf miner larvae create silvery tunnels in young leaves; fruit borer larvae damage developing fruit.
Control measures: Prune and destroy heavily mined shoots on young trees. Spray neem oil during new flush growth. Use pheromone traps for fruit borer.

Issue: Citrus Canker (bacterial)
Symptoms: Raised, corky lesions on leaves, stems, and fruit surrounded by a yellow halo.
Control measures: Use disease-free planting material. Prune and destroy infected plant parts. Apply copper-based bactericide as preventive spray.

--- Papaya ---
Issue: Papaya Mealybug (insect pest)
Symptoms: Cottony white insect masses on leaves, stems, and fruit, causing stunted growth and fruit drop.
Control measures: Release natural predators such as Acerophagus papayae where available. Prune and destroy heavily infested plant parts. Apply neem-based insecticide.

Issue: Papaya Ring Spot Virus
Symptoms: Causes mottled leaves, ring-shaped spots on fruit, and stunted plant growth, spread by aphids.
Control measures: Use tolerant varieties where available. Control aphid vectors. Remove and destroy infected plants early to limit spread.

--- Coconut ---
Issue: Rhinoceros Beetle (insect pest)
Symptoms: Adult beetles bore into the crown, damaging unopened fronds and creating characteristic V-shaped cuts on leaves.
Control measures: Maintain field sanitation by removing decaying organic matter. Apply neem cake or Metarhizium anisopliae biocontrol fungus in the crown. Use pheromone traps.

Issue: Bud Rot (fungal)
Symptoms: Causes rotting of the central growing bud, often fatal if not treated early, worsened by heavy rainfall.
Control measures: Ensure good drainage around the palm base. Remove and destroy affected tissue at first sign. Apply Bordeaux mixture as a protective measure.

--- Cotton ---
Issue: Pink Bollworm (insect pest)
Symptoms: Larvae feed inside cotton bolls, damaging fiber quality and reducing yield.
Control measures: Use Bt cotton varieties for inherent resistance. Install pheromone traps (gossyplure) for monitoring. Destroy infested bolls and crop residue after harvest.

Issue: Whitefly / Jassid (insect pest)
Symptoms: Sap-sucking pests that reduce plant vigor and can transmit leaf curl virus.
Control measures: Use yellow sticky traps. Spray neem-based formulations. Rotate insecticide groups to avoid resistance buildup.

Issue: Bacterial Blight
Symptoms: Causes angular water-soaked lesions on leaves that turn brown/black, can spread to bolls.
Control measures: Use resistant varieties and certified disease-free seed. Avoid overhead irrigation. Remove and destroy infected plant debris.

--- Jute ---
Issue: Jute Semilooper and Stem Weevil (insect pest)
Symptoms: Semilooper caterpillars defoliate leaves; stem weevil larvae bore into stems, weakening fiber quality.
Control measures: Handpick and destroy caterpillars in early stages. Practice field sanitation and crop rotation. Apply insecticide if severe, timed to avoid fiber-quality impact.

Issue: Stem Rot (fungal)
Symptoms: Causes dark lesions on the stem base that can girdle and weaken the plant, reducing fiber yield.
Control measures: Practice crop rotation with non-host crops. Ensure good field drainage. Treat seeds with fungicide before sowing.

--- Coffee ---
Issue: Coffee Berry Borer (insect pest)
Symptoms: Small beetles bore into coffee berries, damaging beans internally and reducing yield and quality.
Control measures: Practice timely and thorough harvesting to remove all berries, including fallen ones. Use alcohol-based traps for monitoring. Apply Beauveria bassiana biocontrol fungus.

Issue: Coffee Leaf Rust (fungal)
Symptoms: Orange-yellow powdery spots on the underside of leaves, causing premature leaf drop and reduced yield.
Control measures: Use rust-resistant varieties (especially important for Arabica). Ensure proper shade management. Apply copper-based fungicide preventively during humid periods.

GENERAL GUIDANCE: For any pest or disease issue not listed here, contact your local Krishi
Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or call the Kisan Call Centre helpline (1800-180-1551).
Always follow pesticide/fungicide label instructions and use protective equipment when applying any chemical control.================================================================================

Market: Coorg, Karnataka. Minimum: Rs 31500/quintal. Maximum: Rs 41500/quintal. Modal (most common): Rs 36500/quintal.
Market: Wayanad, Kerala. Minimum: Rs 32500/quintal. Maximum: Rs 42500/quintal. Modal (most common): Rs 37500/quintal.
Observation: Modal prices for Coffee range from Rs 36500 to Rs 37500 per quintal across these three markets, averaging approximately Rs 37000 per quintal.

GENERAL NOTES:
1. Prices vary by market, season, arrivals, transport cost, and quality grade.
2. Crops with government Minimum Support Price (MSP) - rice, wheat, cotton, maize, chickpea, moong, jute - show less volatility than fruits/vegetables.
3. For real-time prices, farmers should check agmarknet.gov.in or their nearest mandi notice board.
4. When answering price questions, always specify market name and price range, not a single blended figure across unrelated markets.================================================================================

================================================================================
SECTION E: GOVERNMENT SCHEMES AND SUBSIDIES FOR FARMERS
================================================================================
General reference information on major Indian central government agricultural schemes.
Farmers should verify current scheme details, deadlines, and eligibility at pmkisan.gov.in, pmfby.gov.in, or their nearest Common Service Centre (CSC), as scheme rules can change.

Scheme: PM-KISAN (Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Nidhi)
Description: Provides income support of Rs 6000 per year to eligible landholding farmer families, paid in three equal installments of Rs 2000 every four months, directly to bank accounts.
Eligibility and how to apply: All landholding farmer families are eligible, subject to certain exclusion criteria (e.g. institutional landholders, income tax payees in some categories). Registration is done online at pmkisan.gov.in or through the local Common Service Centre, requiring land records and Aadhaar linkage.

Scheme: PMFBY (Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana)
Description: A crop insurance scheme that provides financial support to farmers in case of crop loss/damage due to natural calamities, pests, or diseases. Premium rates are heavily subsidized - farmers pay only 2% for Kharif crops, 1.5% for Rabi crops, and 5% for annual commercial/horticultural crops, with the government covering the remaining premium.
Eligibility and how to apply: Available to all farmers growing notified crops in notified areas, including both loanee and non-loanee farmers. Enrollment typically happens through banks, insurance companies, or the National Crop Insurance Portal, with deadlines tied to the sowing season.

Scheme: KCC (Kisan Credit Card)
Description: Provides farmers with timely access to credit for agricultural and allied activities (crop production, post-harvest expenses, farm maintenance) at subsidized interest rates, often around 4% per annum with prompt repayment incentives.
Eligibility and how to apply: Available to farmers, tenant farmers, and sharecroppers. Application is made through nationalized banks, cooperative banks, or regional rural banks, requiring land documents and identity proof.

Scheme: Soil Health Card Scheme
Description: Provides farmers with a report on their soil's nutrient status (N, P, K, and micronutrients) along with crop-wise fertilizer and soil amendment recommendations, issued once every 2 years per field.
Eligibility and how to apply: Available to all farmers; soil samples are collected by state agriculture department officials or through designated soil testing labs. Farmers can check their card status at soilhealth.dac.gov.in.

Scheme: PMKSY (Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana)
Description: Aims to expand irrigation coverage and improve water use efficiency through micro-irrigation (drip and sprinkler systems), with subsidies covering a significant portion of installation cost for small and marginal farmers.
Eligibility and how to apply: Available to farmers investing in approved micro-irrigation systems; subsidy percentage varies by state and farmer category (higher subsidy typically for small/marginal farmers). Applications are routed through the state horticulture or agriculture department.

Scheme: National Agriculture Market (e-NAM)
Description: An online trading platform connecting existing physical mandis to create a unified national market, allowing farmers to get better price discovery and sell produce beyond their local mandi.
Eligibility and how to apply: Farmers can register at their nearest e-NAM-integrated mandi to access the platform; no separate central registration fee is typically required.

IMPORTANT: Scheme names, amounts, and eligibility rules can change with government policy updates.
Farmers should always verify current details at the official scheme website or their nearest Common Service Centre (CSC) / Krishi Vigyan Kendra before applying.================================================================================
SECTION F: FERTILIZER DOSAGE GENERAL GUIDANCE (BY CROP)
================================================================================
General reference N-P-K fertilizer recommendations based on standard ICAR / State Agricultural
University Package of Practices. ACTUAL DOSAGE SHOULD BE ADJUSTED based on the farmer's own
Soil Health Card report, since soil nutrient status varies significantly by location.
These figures are general starting guidance only, not a substitute for a soil test.

Crop: Rice
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation: 120 kg Nitrogen, 60 kg Phosphorus, 40 kg Potassium per hectare, applied in split doses (basal + tillering + panicle initiation stages). Apply basal dose at transplanting, remaining nitrogen split across 2 top-dressings.

Crop: Maize
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation: 120-150 kg Nitrogen, 60-75 kg Phosphorus, 40-50 kg Potassium per hectare. Apply full phosphorus and potassium plus one-third nitrogen as basal dose; remaining nitrogen split at knee-high and tasseling stages.

Crop: Chickpea
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation: 20 kg Nitrogen (starter dose, as chickpea fixes its own nitrogen), 40-60 kg Phosphorus, 20 kg Potassium per hectare, applied fully as basal dose at sowing.

Crop: Kidneybeans
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation: 20-25 kg Nitrogen (starter dose), 60-80 kg Phosphorus, 30-40 kg Potassium per hectare, applied as basal dose.

Crop: Pigeonpeas
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation: 20-25 kg Nitrogen (starter dose), 50-60 kg Phosphorus, 20-25 kg Potassium per hectare, applied fully at sowing as basal dose.

Crop: Mothbeans
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation: 10-15 kg Nitrogen (starter dose), 20-30 kg Phosphorus per hectare, minimal potassium needed in most arid soils.

Crop: Mungbean
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation: 15-20 kg Nitrogen (starter dose), 40-50 kg Phosphorus, 20 kg Potassium per hectare, applied as basal dose.

Crop: Blackgram
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation: 15-20 kg Nitrogen (starter dose), 40-50 kg Phosphorus, 20 kg Potassium per hectare, applied as basal dose.

Crop: Lentil
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation: 15-20 kg Nitrogen (starter dose), 40-50 kg Phosphorus, 20 kg Potassium per hectare, applied fully at sowing.

Crop: Pomegranate
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation (per mature tree per year): 500-625 g Nitrogen, 250 g Phosphorus, 250 g Potassium, applied in split doses during active growth and pre-flowering stages, plus regular farmyard manure application.

Crop: Banana
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation (per plant per year): 200 g Nitrogen, 60-70 g Phosphorus, 200-300 g Potassium, applied in multiple split doses over the crop cycle, particularly important during shooting/bunch formation stage.

Crop: Mango
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation (per mature tree per year): 1-1.5 kg Nitrogen, 500 g Phosphorus, 1 kg Potassium, applied in split doses before flowering and after fruit set.

Crop: Grapes
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation (per vine per year): varies by training system and age; general guidance is 400-600 g Nitrogen, 200-300 g Phosphorus, 400-600 g Potassium, applied in split doses aligned with pruning and fruiting cycles.

Crop: Watermelon
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation: 80-100 kg Nitrogen, 50-60 kg Phosphorus, 50-60 kg Potassium per hectare, with nitrogen split across basal, vine growth, and flowering stages.

Crop: Muskmelon
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation: 75-100 kg Nitrogen, 50 kg Phosphorus, 50 kg Potassium per hectare, similar split-dose approach to watermelon.

Crop: Apple
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation (per mature tree per year): 500-750 g Nitrogen, 250-375 g Phosphorus, 500-750 g Potassium, applied in early spring before flowering and after fruit set.

Crop: Orange
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation (per mature tree per year): 600-800 g Nitrogen, 200-300 g Phosphorus, 300-400 g Potassium, split across 2-3 applications timed with flowering and fruit development.

Crop: Papaya
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation (per plant per year): 200-250 g Nitrogen, 250 g Phosphorus, 250-500 g Potassium, applied in monthly small split doses throughout the year.

Crop: Coconut
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation (per palm per year): 500 g Nitrogen, 320 g Phosphorus, 1200 g Potassium, applied in 2 split doses, along with organic manure/compost.

Crop: Cotton
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation: 100-120 kg Nitrogen, 50-60 kg Phosphorus, 50-60 kg Potassium per hectare, with nitrogen split across basal, square formation, and flowering stages.

Crop: Jute
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation: 60-80 kg Nitrogen, 30-40 kg Phosphorus, 30-40 kg Potassium per hectare, with nitrogen split between basal and top-dressing about 4-5 weeks after sowing.

Crop: Coffee
Fertilizer guidance: General recommendation (per hectare per year, mature plantation): 150-200 kg Nitrogen, 60-100 kg Phosphorus, 150-200 kg Potassium, applied in 2-3 split doses aligned with monsoon and post-monsoon growth phases.

IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE: Over-application of nitrogen fertilizer can increase susceptibility to certain
pests (e.g. Brown Planthopper in rice) and can also cause environmental runoff issues. Always follow
recommended dosages and consider organic amendments (farmyard manure, compost, green manure) alongside
chemical fertilizers for long-term soil health.================================================================================
SECTION L: IRRIGATION AND WATER MANAGEMENT GUIDANCE (BY CROP)
================================================================================
General irrigation guidance based on standard agricultural extension recommendations.
Actual irrigation scheduling should account for local rainfall, soil water-holding capacity,
and water availability. Drip irrigation is generally recommended for water-scarce regions
and high-value crops to improve water use efficiency.

Crop: Rice
Irrigation guidance: Requires standing water (2.5-5 cm) for most of the vegetative stage; critical irrigation stages are transplanting, tillering, and panicle initiation. Drain field 7-10 days before harvest.

Crop: Maize
Irrigation guidance: Needs irrigation at critical stages: knee-high stage, tasseling/silking, and grain filling. Avoid water stress especially during flowering, as it severely impacts yield.

Crop: Chickpea
Irrigation guidance: Generally grown on residual soil moisture (rainfed); if irrigated, critical stages are pre-flowering and pod development. Avoid excess irrigation, which increases wilt disease risk.

Crop: Kidneybeans
Irrigation guidance: Requires regular but moderate irrigation; critical stages are flowering and pod filling. Avoid waterlogging, which promotes root rot.

Crop: Pigeonpeas
Irrigation guidance: Mostly rainfed; if irrigation is available, critical stages are flowering and pod development. Being deep-rooted, it tolerates moisture stress better than other pulses.

Crop: Mothbeans
Irrigation guidance: Highly drought-tolerant, typically grown without irrigation in arid regions; light irrigation at flowering can improve yield if available.

Crop: Mungbean
Irrigation guidance: Needs light, frequent irrigation; critical stages are flowering and pod filling. Avoid waterlogging.

Crop: Blackgram
Irrigation guidance: Similar to mungbean; needs moderate irrigation at flowering and pod development stages.

Crop: Lentil
Irrigation guidance: Mostly grown on residual moisture; if irrigated, one critical irrigation at pre-flowering stage significantly boosts yield.

Crop: Pomegranate
Irrigation guidance: Drip irrigation is widely recommended; water stress just before flowering can help induce uniform bahar (flowering cycle), but consistent moisture is needed during fruit development to prevent cracking.

Crop: Banana
Irrigation guidance: High water requirement; needs regular irrigation (drip preferred) especially during shooting and bunch formation stages. Water stress causes bunch size reduction.

Crop: Mango
Irrigation guidance: Young trees need regular watering; mature trees benefit from controlled water stress before flowering to induce flowering, followed by consistent irrigation during fruit development.

Crop: Grapes
Irrigation guidance: Drip irrigation is standard practice; irrigation scheduling is tied to pruning cycles, with controlled stress used to manage vine vigor and fruit quality.

Crop: Watermelon
Irrigation guidance: Needs consistent moisture during vine growth and fruit development, but reduce irrigation as fruits mature to improve sweetness.

Crop: Muskmelon
Irrigation guidance: Similar to watermelon; consistent moisture needed until fruit development, then reduced to improve sugar content.

Crop: Apple
Irrigation guidance: Needs regular irrigation especially during fruit development in dry hill regions; drip or basin irrigation is common depending on terrain.

Crop: Orange
Irrigation guidance: Regular irrigation needed, especially during flowering and fruit development; avoid water stress during these critical stages to prevent fruit drop.

Crop: Papaya
Irrigation guidance: Needs regular, frequent irrigation as it has shallow roots and low drought tolerance; drip irrigation is well-suited.

Crop: Coconut
Irrigation guidance: Deep-rooted and moderately drought-tolerant once mature, but young palms need regular watering; basin irrigation is common in traditional systems.

Crop: Cotton
Irrigation guidance: Needs irrigation at square formation, flowering, and boll development stages; water stress during flowering significantly reduces yield.

Crop: Jute
Irrigation guidance: Needs consistent moisture during vegetative growth; typically grown in areas with adequate rainfall or near water bodies given its high water requirement.

Crop: Coffee
Irrigation guidance: Needs supplemental irrigation in drier regions, particularly to induce uniform flowering after the dry season (blossom showers) and during berry development.

GENERAL WATER-SAVING TIPS:
1. Use drip or sprinkler irrigation where feasible to reduce water wastage compared to flood irrigation.
2. Irrigate during early morning or evening to reduce evaporation losses.
3. Mulching around plants helps retain soil moisture and reduce irrigation frequency.
4. Monitor soil moisture rather than irrigating on a fixed schedule, where possible.
5. PMKSY (Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana) offers subsidies for micro-irrigation systems - see government schemes section.================================================================================
SECTION G: GLOSSARY OF AGRICULTURAL TERMS
================================================================================

Kharif: The monsoon cropping season in India, roughly June to October, when crops are sown with the onset of monsoon rains.
Rabi: The winter cropping season in India, roughly November to March, when crops are sown after the monsoon retreats and grown using residual soil moisture and irrigation.
Zaid: A short summer cropping season between the Rabi harvest and Kharif sowing, roughly April to May, usually requiring irrigation.
MSP (Minimum Support Price): A government-guaranteed minimum price at which certain crops are purchased from farmers, protecting them from price crashes during bumper harvests.
Mandi: A wholesale agricultural market, typically regulated under state Agricultural Produce Market Committee (APMC) laws, where farmers sell their produce.
Quintal: A unit of mass equal to 100 kilograms, commonly used in Indian agricultural trade and pricing.
N-P-K: Shorthand for the three primary plant nutrients: Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), and Potassium (K), the basis of most fertilizer recommendations.
Soil pH: A measure of soil acidity or alkalinity on a scale of 0-14, where 7 is neutral; most crops grow best in a specific pH range.
Economic Threshold Level (ETL): The pest population level at which the cost of crop damage equals the cost of control, used to decide when pesticide application becomes worthwhile.
Biocontrol / Biopesticide: Pest control methods using living organisms (like Trichogramma wasps) or naturally derived substances (like neem oil) instead of synthetic chemical pesticides.
Bt Cotton: A genetically modified cotton variety containing a gene from the bacterium Bacillus thuringiensis, providing built-in resistance to certain bollworm pests.
Intercropping: Growing two or more crops simultaneously in the same field to improve pest resistance, soil fertility, and land-use efficiency.
Crop Rotation: Growing different types of crops in the same field across different seasons to break pest/disease cycles and maintain soil fertility.
Farmyard Manure (FYM): Organic manure made from decomposed animal dung, urine, and bedding material, used to improve soil structure and fertility.
Bund: A raised earthen boundary around a field, especially rice fields, used to retain water and prevent soil erosion.
KVK (Krishi Vigyan Kendra): A district-level farm science center under ICAR that provides local agricultural extension services, training, and advice to farmers.
Agmarknet: The Indian government's online portal (agmarknet.gov.in) providing daily wholesale price information for agricultural commodities across mandis.
IMD (India Meteorological Department): India's national weather forecasting agency under the Ministry of Earth Sciences, providing official weather bulletins and warnings.
Bahar: A term used in fruit cultivation (especially pomegranate) referring to a managed flowering/fruiting cycle, timed by farmers through irrigation and pruning practices.

================================================================================
SECTION H: FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS (FAQ)
================================================================================

Q: What is the best crop for sandy soil with low rainfall?
A: Crops like mothbeans, mungbean, and blackgram are generally well-suited to sandy soils with lower rainfall, as they are drought-tolerant. Check the soil/climate suitability section for exact nutrient and rainfall ranges for each crop.

Q: How do I know if my soil pH is suitable for a crop?
A: Check the crop's soil and climate suitability entry in this dataset for its typical pH range, and compare it with your own Soil Health Card report, which lists your field's actual pH.

Q: What should I do if I see pest damage but I'm not sure which pest it is?
A: Compare the visible symptoms (e.g. leaf tunneling, sap-sucking yellowing, boring into fruit/pods) against the pest and disease entries for your crop in this dataset. If still unsure, contact your local Krishi Vigyan Kendra for identification.

Q: Are the mandi prices in this dataset updated daily?
A: No. The mandi prices in this dataset are sample/illustrative reference values from a mid-2026 snapshot. Real mandi prices change daily; check agmarknet.gov.in or your local mandi for current rates.

Q: What is the difference between Kharif, Rabi, and Zaid season?
A: Kharif is the monsoon season (June-October), Rabi is the winter season (November-March), and Zaid is a short summer season (April-May) usually requiring irrigation. See the glossary section for full definitions.

Q: How much fertilizer should I apply to my field?
A: This dataset provides general fertilizer dosage guidance per crop, but actual dosage should be adjusted based on your specific Soil Health Card report, since soil nutrient status varies by location.

Q: What government schemes can help me financially?
A: Key schemes include PM-KISAN (direct income support), PMFBY (crop insurance), Kisan Credit Card (subsidized credit), Soil Health Card (soil testing), and PMKSY (irrigation subsidy). See the government schemes section for details.

Q: Is this weather forecast still valid?
A: The weather data in this dataset is a static snapshot from an IMD bulletin issued 11 July 2026, covering forecasts through 17 July 2026. For current weather, check mausam.imd.gov.in.

Q: What should I do during a heavy rainfall warning?
A: Ensure proper field drainage to prevent waterlogging, postpone irrigation and pesticide/fertilizer application until the sky clears, and harvest any matured crops before the rain intensifies if possible.

Q: Can I grow fruits like mango or grapes in any season?
A: Most fruit trees and vines (mango, grapes, banana, coconut, papaya, orange, apple) are perennial crops - they are planted once and produce over multiple years, with flowering/harvest tied to their own biological cycle rather than a single planting season.

GENERAL MONSOON STATUS (as of bulletin date):
The Southwest Monsoon covered the entire country on 9 July 2026.
The Monsoon trough at mean sea level was passing through Sri Ganganagar, Rohtak, Shahjahanpur, Basti, and Muzaffarpur, extending east-southeastwards toward Manipur.

GENERAL AGROMET ADVISORY - THUNDERSTORM / GUSTY WIND CONDITIONS (applicable nationwide when alerts are active):
Shift harvested produce to safer, covered locations or cover with tarpaulin sheets to protect from strong winds.
Tie down harvested crops securely to minimize displacement risk.
Provide mechanical support (staking) to vegetables and young fruit-bearing plants to prevent lodging in strong winds.

GENERAL LIVESTOCK / POULTRY / FISHERIES ADVISORY (applicable during heavy rainfall or heat wave conditions):
Keep animals sheltered during heavy rainfall and provide balanced feed.
Store feed and fodder in a dry, safe location to prevent spoilage.
During heat wave conditions, provide clean, cool drinking water to animals and cover poultry shed roofs with grass/thatch to reduce heat stress.
Maintain proper netting around fish ponds to prevent escape during overflow from heavy rain.

---

WEATHER TERMINOLOGY REFERENCE:
Widespread (WS): Rain reported at 76-100% of stations in a region.
Fairly Widespread (FWS): Rain reported at 51-75% of stations.
Scattered (SCT): Rain reported at 26-50% of stations.
Isolated (ISOL): Rain reported at 1-25% of stations.
Dry: No rain reported.
Heat Wave: Maximum temperature departure of 4.5-6.4°C above normal, or actual temperature ≥45°C (plains).
Heavy Rainfall: 64.5 to 115.5 mm in 24 hours.
Very Heavy Rainfall: 115.6 to 204.4 mm in 24 hours.
Extremely Heavy Rainfall: More than 204.4 mm in 24 hours.

SOURCE NOTE: Paraphrased and restructured from the IMD "All India Weather Summary and Forecast Bulletin" dated 11 July 2026. For live, region-specific forecasts, refer to https://mausam.imd.gov.in or contact IMD at 011-2434-4599.
================================================================================
SECTION K: PEST CONTROL QUICK REFERENCE SUMMARY (one entry per crop)
================================================================================

Rice: Yellow Stem Borer causes 'dead heart' and 'white ear'. Control: light traps, Trichogramma release, Chlorantraniliprole if threshold crossed.
Maize: Fall Armyworm damages whorl leaves and cobs. Control: intercrop with legumes, pheromone traps, Bt or Spinetoram-based insecticide if severe.
Chickpea: Gram Pod Borer bores into pods. Control: pheromone traps, NPV biopesticide or insecticide at early larval stage.
Kidneybeans: Aphids and Whitefly cause leaf curling. Control: yellow sticky traps, neem-based spray.
Pigeonpeas: Gram Pod Borer and Pod Fly damage pods/seeds. Control: pheromone traps, timely sowing.
Mothbeans: Aphids cause stunted growth. Control: neem oil spray, encourage beneficial insects.
Mungbean: Thrips and Whitefly cause flower shedding. Control: sticky traps, neem spray.
Blackgram: Bihar Hairy Caterpillar causes defoliation. Control: handpick egg masses, light traps.
Lentil: Black Aphid causes leaf curling. Control: encourage predators, neem oil.
Pomegranate: Fruit Borer causes internal rot. Control: bag fruits early, pheromone traps.
Banana: Weevil Borer and Nematodes damage pseudostem/roots. Control: disease-free suckers, neem cake.
Mango: Mango Hopper causes sooty mold. Control: prune canopy, neem oil during flowering.
Grapes: Mealybugs weaken vines. Control: sticky bands on trunks, natural predators.
Watermelon: Fruit Fly causes internal rot. Control: methyl eugenol traps, field sanitation.
Muskmelon: Fruit Fly and Aphids damage fruit. Control: fruit fly traps, neem oil.
Apple: Woolly Apple Aphid causes galls. Control: prune infested shoots, dormant-season insecticide.
Orange: Citrus Leaf Miner damages leaves. Control: prune mined shoots, neem oil.
Papaya: Papaya Mealybug causes stunted growth. Control: release natural predators, neem-based insecticide.
Coconut: Rhinoceros Beetle bores into crown. Control: field sanitation, neem cake, pheromone traps.
Cotton: Pink Bollworm damages bolls. Control: Bt cotton, gossyplure traps, Spinosad if severe.
Jute: Jute Semilooper and Stem Weevil weaken stems. Control: handpick caterpillars, field sanitation.
Coffee: Coffee Berry Borer damages beans. Control: thorough harvesting, alcohol traps, Beauveria bassiana.
